speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 5 with Erwin Chaim, October 25, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were, slowly but surely, selling some of our belongings. The dental supply was one of the items that we sold. We were able to get out some oil paintings, which was strictly contraband, but which we took out. One of them was a copy, a copy. A matter of fact, it was similar to the portrait of the Mona Lisa. And he was trying to sell this to an art dealer. Due to my father's being very bright, he smelled a rat. They were going to mug him and take his painting away. Somehow, he was able to get the painting away from there, and ran and got out of there, and came back to Hongkew with a bloody nose, I think, or something. But he had the painting. He was able, at a later date, to-- to do-- sell that painting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you able to leave this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. The-- the ghetto wasn't a ghetto, til a year after the Japanese-- til Pearl Harbor, til the Japanese declared war on the United States. And the only-- I think, one of the only reasons-- the Japanese wouldn't have put us into this camp. It was the Germans, some of the Nazis that lived in Shanghai, I think, that forced that particular thing. I'm not really sure of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was the-- was your home inside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did you have to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was right in the center of-- of that community. Yes-- no, we did not have to move. And so a lot-- lot of the Jewish population, a lot of the Jewish refugees lived in Hongkew, but worked in the city. See, we were not in the city. We were the suburbs. The city was the international settlement, the British concession, and the French town. These were actually, at one time, administered by the French, the British, and the Americans in the international settlement. They had large, tall buildings for Shanghai, 15, 20 stories high. They had a race course in the middle of the city. They had a streetcar. They had buses, restaurants. And yes, a lot of our refugees had factories, and shops, and everything, and supported-- and supported the rest of the community that was not able to make a living, by-- by their being industrious. This all came to a seizing stop, when the Japanese says, no. You have to move into the Hongkew ghetto. The ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: As a 12-year-old, did you know what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah. By that-- by that time, I was 15. Things then started to become rough. You take the livelihood away from these people, then your in-- your industrial base, your financial base, is-- everything is gone. I mean, we lived in between the Chinese people. And the Chinese people had stores. And we bought, you know, fruits and vegetable, meat, if we could afford it. As an aside, for the seven years that we lived in Shanghai, you did not want to drink water that wasn't cooked-- boiled. You did not want to eat any fruit that wasn't cooked. The Chinese fertilized their fields with human excrement, and you get diarrhea. And when you get the diarrhea in Shanghai, you just about can kiss yourself goodbye. The one thing that we did not have, became in short supply, and shorter and shorter, was medical-- medical-- medications. They were just not there. So you tried your darndest not to try to get dysentery. I got a light case of it once.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the Jewish factories, stores, that were outside to ghettos were confiscated by the-- the Japanese. And you had to move into the ghetto. One of the subterfuges that you maybe could do is, if you had a Chinese partner, you were able to continue with the business. What usually happened, the Chinese then said, forget it, and never sent any money towards the refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: The interesting thing, like I said before, the Chinese-- excuse me-- --were not the ones that led us into Shanghai. The Chinese really didn't like us, as much as the Japanese tolerated us. I don't know about liking us, but tolerated us, better than the Chinese.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1942, when it finally came to the ghetto, things started to become really bad. There was no money. And groceries, because Shanghai-- greater Shanghai, was cut off from the rest of China. You know, the Japanese were at war with the Chinese. And coffee, tea-- well, all provisions became scarcer and scarcer. And of course, the price became higher and higher. My mother took up smoking in Shanghai, to kind of still the hunger pains.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember going to the grocery stores. You buy a quarter of an ounce of sausage. You buy a half an ounce of coffee. [LAUGHS] Refrigeration, of course, was not heard of. Nobody had refrigeration, not even ice. So you had to do your grocery shopping every day. It became so bad, coffee was one of the luxuries that, apparently, my parents really wanted to have. Well, it got to the point where we couldn't afford coffee anymore. So we had a friend, who worked in one of the coffee shops. Now, remember what I said before. There's people who didn't have any money. And even under the worst circumstances, there's always people who have more money than the others. And there were people who were still drinking coffee. Well, this friend of ours sold us the coffee grounds. We then took the coffee grounds, boiled the coffee grounds, and had coffee again, the second time around. Later on, in-- here in Denver, we had a cleaning woman, who was not Jewish, who lived in the war in Czechoslovakia. We told her that story once. And she said to us, oh, that was nothing. We ate the coffee grounds-- mixed it with something else. So you know, it goes on, and on, and on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So things became rougher, and rougher, and rougher. And shortages everywhere. And one of the things that my father-- again being the smart guy that he was, saw the handwriting on the wall-- was that fuel is going to be really in short supply. I mean, you had to cook your food with something.

speaker: interviewee
text: In China, you cooked your food on a charcoal stove, which was on the inside of your house, which gave off carbon monoxide gases. [LAUGHS] And a lot of people got sick of that. We had our stove, kind of, in the window. Being that my father was a dentist, he was able to get electricity to power his electric drill and his polishing machine. At that time, there was a difference made between the electricity that you used for lights and the electricity to use for motors. One of them was called electricity, other one was called power.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had two electric meters. Therefore, when they started rationing electricity, we had like a double ration. And it was enough for us to do some of our warming up and cooking on an electric hot plate. But there wasn't enough electricity there to waste, shall we say, to light our room at night. We did have a radio. The radio was our lifeline because, in Shanghai, being that it was an international city as it was, they had a communist-- a Russian radio station, which was allowed to broadcast. We had a German radio station, which was allowed to broadcast. You can imagine the conflicting news broadcasts of the Russian Front and the German Front. They never matched, you know? But we also had music-- Chinese music, if you wanted it, Russian music, or German music.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, to make a long story short, being that electricity was rationed, I took that radio apart and took the, little tiny dial, lamp that's behind it, and mounted it on the outside, so that we could have lights in our room. Just a tiny little bulb. It was enough for us to sit there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was around 14, 15, maybe 16. And I was always handy with my hands. I learned it in [? art, ?] and electricity was second nature to me. We also had a petroleum stove and heater. And you needed petroleum to heat it with. My father bought a supply of, I believe it was, 10 tins, as they were called-- five gallon metal cans that were sealed, soldered shut, to use as fuel. Well, it turned out that that was liquid gold. The price of that petroleum became absolutely sky high. And my father said, we can't afford to use it for as our own fuel, we have to sell it to make a living. And so those are those sort of things. My father was able to finagle himself in such a way that, I will say, never starved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. We were the Three Musketeers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kurt Jacoby, who lives in Chicago, Heinz Wasserman, who lives in California, close to Los Angeles, and myself, who lives in-- in-- in Denver, Colorado, we were the three buddies. My sister also had three girlfriends, and they didn't have any special names. But we were-- we were called The Three Musketeers. And we did a lot of things together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you kept in touch since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. All three of us are still alive and are doing quite well. My-- my buddy, Kurt Jacoby, became partners in a metal-making factory. And Heinz Wasserman became a very sought-after consultant in-- in California. I forgot exactly what he was. But he was ready to retire, and his-- his bosses wouldn't let him retire. So he had to work, I think, for another three or four years with big bonuses. Now, he's retired. And-- and-- and by the way, I am. I am too. We'll come to that later, what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me where you were and the circumstances when the war was coming to an end.

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the business-- yeah. The business of a radio. Now, we were not allowed, of course, to have short wave radios. Anything that was broadcast in Shanghai, that was OK. But short wave radios-- in other words, trying to find out what-- what's happening in the United States. Naturally, we did, actually, in Shanghai, among our Jewish more military youth, had an underground, who tried a little sabotage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a part of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I was not part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- did you know about it then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, not. Basically, not. And I don't really think they did much of anything, but they did have a-- a shortwave radio. And the news was spread that the war is over. The war is over [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] between the Japanese and the United States. You know, come to think of it, the war in Germany was over first. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't think we knew that. This, I don't-- I-- I-- I-- I have no-- I have no recollection of that. I do have recollections of the Japanese-American War. Well, a bunch of that military group-- the name escapes me, they did have a name-- marched onto the Japanese police station and politely asked for them to surrender. They did. They pulled down the Japanese flag and raised the Star of David, and sat in the police station. Oh, they put those Japanese people in a-- in a back room.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four hours later, a company of Japanese soldiers came to the Front and said, you know, guys, the war isn't over. Go home. Pulled down the Jewish flag, raised the Japanese flag again, and everything became back to normal. Nobody got arrested. No-- no-- nobody got into a fight. [LAUGHS] It was just like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: A number of days later, we heard some commotions, and the war was over. And the Japanese just left. And then, after a while, the Americans came. Do you know, all those Americans were 10 foot tall and 300 pounds heavy? And we were the little guys, undernourished and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before we get to that, there was one very sad incident that happened, of course, to the people in Hongkew. They had an-- an administrator who was supposed to administer the Jews of the ghetto in Hongkew. His name was Goya, and he was a lunatic, an absolute Jew-hater and a lunatic. He was very short, even shorter than most Japanese at that time were. And he was to king of the Jews. And he mistreated us, very fiercely. You could get permission to leave the ghetto. The ghetto didn't have any walls or barbed wired around it, but there was a bounty on all the refugees, the white people, compared to the yellow people, the Chinese.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a definite-- you could not blend into a Chinese population. And if you were caught outside, there was a bounty on our heads. And as most Chinese were very poor, hey, they'd grab you. OK? So you didn't do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you were able to get a button, or a passport, to go, maybe to your place of work or something. There was some ways to get out of the ghetto and to do some work outside, in-- in the city. But in order to get that passport, you had to get through a lot of indignities. He slapped you. He insulted you. And if worse comes to worst, he'd put you in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did have a contact, because I had a job at one time as a mechanic, outside of the Hongkew ghetto. It turned out that the guy that hired me was a German. I could not enter his place of business through the front door. I was an employee. I had to go to through the back door. At big Nazi flag insignia was on the back door, which I had to go through every day. I could not say, 'OK.' I had to say, 'Jawohl.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, sir. Not OK, that's American. I did, of course, speak a little English, whatever, in-- in Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you learn Chinese also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. I learned some Chinese, broken-- broken Chinese. Able to communicate. But we needed the money for that-- what little money I made. Unfortunately, I was told that the water has a-- that comes out the faucet-- had a water filter on it, which I saw. And you can drink the water. Well, the filter was defective, and I got a case of dysentery. This was very much at the end-- at the end the war. And I got a case of dysentery. And I immediately got-- got laid off. All right. So I had this case of dysentery. I got laid off, which meant I had to return the passport. I was late, because I was sick. And nobody else could turn that passport in. You had to stay in line, in the burning sun. The line was like, maybe, a block long. And you waited four or five hours. Temperature in the sun, 120 degrees, humidity, 95, you know, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had to stay in line. And of course, you are nervous. Boy, you are scared. What's going to happen to me? Is he going to slap me? Is he going to put me in jail? Who knows. As it turned out, I waited in line. I remember getting to the front of the line. What's your problem? And I told him that I had a job outside. I got dysentery. I was at home, sick. I couldn't come. I would like to turn in my pass. Where's your pass? Here it is. Give it to me. I gave it to them. OK, I didn't even see Goya, just some flunky in the front. So that was my experience, as far as is concerned. But there are some-- some horror stories about that. So tell me about your-- as the war was over, and-- and you were liberated, tell me how you and your family ended up leaving Shanghai.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The war was over, and now the doors started opening. And lo and behold, the American consul started calling the numbers. Come, you have a number, you have a visa, OK? [LAUGHS] Get ready to go to the United States. And this is when we were called, and we did not have any papers. And we were able to get the papers through Senator Hirschfield. And nothing stood in the way of leaving Shanghai. The situation, then, is such that, of course, the Japanese had to give up. They were conquered. The Chinese, then, well-- won the war at the same time that Japan surrendered to the United States. And the communistic party became very active in China and in Shanghai. And the Jews, all of a sudden, again, became the enemy of the state. And we, again, had some altercations with some Chinese youth, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, specifically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. We-- we took precautions to the fact that you didn't go alone anymore, basically, outside the-- the-- the designated-- we-- we still lived in what was the former ghetto. And a lot of people, who still had some money, maybe, went back into town. And I got a job with the United States government as a carpenter. And I was wearing American clothes with-- American military clothes, without any insignia. And I did have a-- a pass. Being that I was now an employee, I was able to ride their buses, which were more like-- like trucks with benches. So we were able to go into town and in-- well, The Three Musketeers. And no, we personally had no altercation with the Chinese. We just stayed away. We just stayed away from that-- from that part.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically speaking, we gathered our belongings, what little was left, and started to pack, and left Shanghai in April of 1947, on the I forgot the name now. What was the name of it? It was a troop ship. The troop ship was fixed up with bunks. The men were in the forward part of the ship, and all the women and children were in the back part of the ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the was a troop ship, they had four bunks. And when it became a civilian troop ship, there was only three, which meant that the 18 days that we were-- it took 18 days to get to the United States-- we were able actually to turn around in our bunk. Which I understand, during the war, when they had troop ships, it was four aboard. You just laid flat. You couldn't turn around. I gained one pound a day on board ship. We had very good food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you weigh when you left? I-- I don't remember. All I know is that I gained a pound a day. I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also remember there were some Chinese people on board. And we weren't even out of the harbor, the Yangtze River, the coast of China, when we had chow time. And as the Chinese are always a bunch of pigs, as far as I'm concerned, tried to crowd into the line in front of the refugees. Well now, hey, wait a minute. Now, we are not refugees anymore. We are not in China anymore. We are going to the United States, and you better get to the back of the line. And we had a huge fight, a bad, huge, bloody battle. Put the Chinese finally in place.

speaker: interviewer
text: We never saw the Chinese again. They were banned. They got food in their special compartment where they lived. And frankly speaking, I don't think I saw a Chinese at all during the 18 days. Was it 15 or 18 days? I don't exactly remember-- our passage to the United States. Did you do anything special on your--

speaker: interviewee
text: We certainly did. It just so happens that the feast of Passover, Pesach, was-- the date was on board ship. We left Shanghai, just like the Jews left Israel to go to the promised land. And we left Shanghai to go to the promised land. And we had-- some of the people knew about this and had matzoh on board. We had haggadahs, furnished by the Armed Forces on board. And we actually had the first night and the second night Seder on board, with white tablecloths. There actually was an altercation. The dining room was also used for showing movies at night. Those two nights, there weren't any movies, and some people complained. You know how it is. But those were very, very nice days aboard ship. Had a good time. Had good weather. I understand there were some of these ships, at a later date, that had to work in the kitchen. They actually had to paint some of the superstructure. We were passengers, and we had a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived 15 to 18 days later in-- the date escaped me, but it was April of 1947, in San Francisco. The last night on board, we stayed up. We had to see the Golden Gate Bridge, which we saw at about 4 or 5 o'clock in the morning. And it was golden. And then, we landed in one of the cays in-- in San Francisco. We were met, again, by the American Allied Campaign Society, and we were given quarters in hotels.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll change tapes right here.
